options.text: Commence nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg via oxygen-driven nebuliser, reassess work of breathing at 20 minutes
Admit for supportive care with oxygen titrated to SpO2 of at least 92% and nasogastric feeds at two-thirds maintenance
Single dose of oral dexamethasone 3.6 mg and arrange GP follow-up with safety-net advice in 24 hours
Commence IV ceftriaxone 300 mg empirically for presumed secondary bacterial lower respiratory tract infection in this infant
Discharge home on room air with written safety-net advice and arrange GP review within the next 24 hours
Subcutaneous adrenaline 220 microg into the lateral thigh, reassess and repeat in 5 minutes if no response
Intravenous magnesium sulfate 1.1 g (max 2 g) over 20 minutes with continuous cardiac and BP monitoring
Rapid sequence intubation and mechanical ventilation with permissive hypercapnia in the emergency department now
Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg via oxygen-driven nebuliser and observe for stridor reduction over 30 minutes
Switch from IV hydrocortisone to oral prednisolone 22 mg and continue back-to-back nebulised salbutamol
Single dose of oral dexamethasone 2 mg alone, then discharge home with written croup advice
Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg plus oral prednisolone 13 mg, reassess work of breathing in 30 minutes
Oral dexamethasone 8 mg plus nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000, observe at least 4 hours
Urgent lateral neck radiograph in the ED to exclude epiglottitis before any pharmacological therapy is commenced
Intramuscular ceftriaxone 650 mg stat and call anaesthetics urgently for theatre intubation under inhalational induction
Oral amoxicillin 375 mg three times daily for 5 days with safety-net advice and review in 48 hours
Oral azithromycin 150 mg once daily for 5 days to cover atypical organisms such as Mycoplasma pneumoniae
Refer immediately to ED for IV benzylpenicillin 900 mg and admission for parenteral therapy and observation
Arrange urgent outpatient chest x-ray before starting any antibiotic therapy to confirm radiological consolidation today
Symptomatic management with regular oral paracetamol only, no antibiotics, and review in the clinic in 48 hours
IV ceftriaxone monotherapy now; defer LP until CRP is back to decide whether it is needed
IV benzylpenicillin plus IV gentamicin now; defer LP until after antibiotics if the infant is too unwell to safely lie flat
Lumbar puncture first, then IV benzylpenicillin plus IV gentamicin plus IV cefotaxime once CSF is obtained
Await CRP and FBC results before starting antibiotics; perform LP only if CRP is over 40 mg/L
Oral amoxicillin three times daily plus admission for observation; reserve LP for clinical deterioration
IV dexamethasone then IV ceftriaxone plus IV vancomycin, with neurosurgery referral
IV aciclovir empirically for HSV encephalitis and defer all empirical antibacterial therapy until LP is performed
IV ceftriaxone monotherapy without vancomycin or dexamethasone, then proceed directly to LP before any imaging
Commence quadruple anti-tuberculous therapy and IV ceftriaxone now, deferring corticosteroid until TB PCR result returns positive
Perform LP immediately before any imaging or antibiotic to ensure maximum microbiological yield from CSF cultures today
Arrange a micturating cystourethrogram (MCUG) within 6 weeks to assess for vesicoureteric reflux and posterior urethral valves
Arrange a renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks and consider further imaging only if abnormal
No further imaging required as the urinary tract infection has resolved with oral antibiotic therapy
Commence long-term prophylactic trimethoprim nocte for 12 months pending nephrology outpatient review
Refer urgently to paediatric urology for outpatient cystoscopy and biopsy within the next 6 weeks
Commence IV 0.9% sodium chloride bolus 240 mL over 15 minutes and admit for ongoing rehydration
Discharge home on ad-lib oral fluids of choice and review by the local GP within 24 hours
Commence nasogastric oral rehydration solution at 300 mL/hr for 4 hours plus single dose oral ondansetron
Commence empirical oral azithromycin 120 mg daily for presumed bacterial gastroenteritis and continue oral rehydration at home
Commence oral loperamide 1.2 mg to slow stool frequency and discharge home with safety-net advice today
Commence IV 0.9% sodium chloride with potassium at 1.5x maintenance, withhold feeds, refer surgery for pyloromyotomy
Refer immediately to theatre for urgent pyloromyotomy now before any electrolyte correction or nasogastric decompression is undertaken
Commence oral erythromycin 36 mg four times daily as prokinetic therapy and continue normal volume feeds today
Trial a thickened anti-reflux infant formula for 1 week with PRN paracetamol and no further investigation or imaging
Arrange an outpatient upper GI contrast study and gastroenterology review in 1 week with continued oral feeds at home
Give a third dose of IV midazolam 3 mg now and repeat at 5 minutes if seizure activity persists
Load with IV levetiracetam 760 mg over 5 minutes with cardiac monitoring, then prepare RSI with thiopentone
Load with IV sodium valproate 760 mg given that he is already on this medication for his epilepsy
Administer IV magnesium sulfate 950 mg over 20 minutes as second-line anticonvulsant therapy for refractory status
Give IV 10% dextrose 38 mL empirically for possible hypoglycaemia and reassess seizure activity in 5 minutes
IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg single infusion plus high-dose aspirin until afebrile for 48 hours, then switch to low-dose antiplatelet aspirin until inflammatory markers and echocardiogram are normal
IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg plus oral prednisolone as primary adjunct; withhold aspirin because of risk of Reye syndrome in a child under 5
Oral amoxicillin three times daily for 10 days for presumed group A streptococcal scarlet fever
Defer IVIG until echocardiography is completed and confirms coronary changes, then treat
Reassurance and antipyretics; arrange paediatric outpatient review if fever persists beyond day 10

rewrites._note: IVIG dose retained because 2 g/kg vs alternative IVIG dosing is the actual clinical anchor for KD; aspirin per-kg/day stripped to avoid the asymmetric cue with C and pediatric short doses.

options.text: Discharge home with paracetamol PRN only, no urinalysis or blood pressure monitoring, and review in 2 weeks if rash persists
Commence oral prednisolone for 4 weeks as routine first-line therapy regardless of severity or renal involvement
Refer urgently to paediatric surgery for laparotomy to exclude ileoileal intussusception before further outpatient review or investigation
Paracetamol/NSAID for pain, weekly urinalysis and BP for 6 months, prompt review for new haematuria or proteinuria
Commence aspirin as an anti-platelet agent and review urinalysis and blood pressure monthly for 6 months
Reassure the family that this is benign physiological jaundice not requiring intervention and review by midwife in 48 hours
Plot bilirubin on age-in-hours nomogram and commence intensive phototherapy if at or above treatment line
Stop breastfeeding immediately for 48 hours, switch to exclusive infant formula feeds, and recheck serum bilirubin level tomorrow
Arrange exchange transfusion now in the neonatal unit irrespective of nomogram position or response to commenced phototherapy
Commence oral phenobarbitone to induce hepatic glucuronyl transferase conjugation and review bilirubin in 48 hours
Commence empirical oral iron supplementation in the community and review the full blood count in 4 weeks today
Transfuse packed red blood cells and platelets in the GP rooms today before referral to a tertiary paediatric centre
Refer urgently same-day to paediatric oncology for bone marrow aspirate, tumour lysis prevention, supportive care
Commence oral prednisolone pending paediatric oncology review at the tertiary centre this week as bridging therapy
Arrange routine outpatient haematology review within 2 weeks and continue conservative observation with weekly bloods at the GP
Give IV hydrocortisone 100 mg as primary treatment now and observe for 30 minutes before any further treatment escalation
Repeat IM adrenaline 220 microg, give 440 mL saline bolus, then start adrenaline infusion if no response
Switch to IV adrenaline bolus 220 microg as initial escalation and repeat every 5 minutes as needed for ongoing shock
Give nebulised salbutamol 5 mg as primary treatment for the bronchospasm and hold further IM adrenaline pending response
Give an additional dose of oral cetirizine 10 mg, observe for 30 minutes and reassess vital signs before any escalation
Intravenous diazepam 5 mg slow push, repeat at 5 minutes
Intravenous phenytoin loading 1 g over 20 minutes
Intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g loading then 1 g/hour maintenance
Intravenous levetiracetam 1.5 g over 5 minutes as load
Immediate Caesarean section under general anaesthesia without anticonvulsant therapy
IM/IV ergometrine 250 mcg, repeatable up to 5 doses over 2 hours as uterotonic
IM carboprost (15-methyl PGF2alpha) 250 mcg, repeatable up to 8 doses
Misoprostol 200 mcg PO single dose, no further uterotonic added at this stage
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV only, with no further uterotonic added at this stage
Recombinant factor VIIa 5 mg IV bolus without further uterotonic at this stage
Proceed with LP now and start IV ceftriaxone plus dexamethasone after CSF
Give IV ceftriaxone at the standard sepsis dose, defer LP, and arrange urgent neuroimaging only after antibiotics are running
Give IV dexamethasone then IV ceftriaxone at the meningitis dose, defer LP, arrange CT brain after stabilisation
Give IV benzylpenicillin alone, defer LP, and observe in resuscitation for 30 minutes
Perform CT brain first, then LP if the scan is normal, then commence antibiotics once CSF is obtained
Commence IV aciclovir at the paediatric encephalitis dose q8h empirically while awaiting CSF HSV PCR
Commence high-dose IV methylprednisolone for presumed anti-NMDAR encephalitis pending antibody results today
Commence IV ceftriaxone and dexamethasone, withhold antivirals until PCR results
Arrange urgent EEG and defer all therapy until non-convulsive status epilepticus is fully excluded
Commence IV ribavirin for suspected Japanese encephalitis given the recent Murray Valley exposure risk
Oral roxithromycin twice daily for 7 days continuously
Oral azithromycin once daily for 5 days, admit for cardiorespiratory monitoring
Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole twice daily for 14 days
IV ceftriaxone once daily for 7 days continuously
Withhold antibiotics; they do not change clinical course once paroxysms have begun
Repeat the Mantoux in 8 weeks because 12 mm without BCG may be non-specific
Reassure that the Mantoux is negative because it is under 15 mm and discharge with no further investigation
Order IGRA (QuantiFERON or T-SPOT) instead because it is more specific and avoid further skin testing
Start a 6-month course of rifampicin monotherapy without isoniazid today
Diagnose latent TB and commence isoniazid daily for 9 months with pyridoxine
Give intramuscular normal human immunoglobulin (NHIG) because she is under 9 months
Administer MMR now (within 72 hours of exposure), notify public health, repeat MMR at 18 months
Withhold post-exposure prophylaxis because she is currently asymptomatic; arrange measles serology day 14
Administer MMR vaccine plus oral ribavirin for 7 days starting today
Reassure that maternal antibody will protect her until she is 12 months old
Live attenuated varicella vaccine 0.5 mL subcutaneously within 5 days of exposure
Oral valaciclovir q8h for 7 days starting from day 7 after exposure
Oral aciclovir six-hourly for 5 days starting immediately today
Observation only and IV aciclovir q8h if a rash develops
Zoster immunoglobulin (VZIG) intramuscularly within 96 hours of exposure (up to 10 days)
Regular analgesia (paracetamol with or without ibuprofen) and safety-net advice to return or recommence antibiotics if not improving at 48 to 72 hours
Immediate oral amoxicillin at half the standard AOM dose three times daily for 5 days
Immediate oral amoxicillin-clavulanate twice daily for 7 days
Immediate oral cefaclor three times daily for 7 days
Topical ciprofloxacin 0.3% ear drops twice daily for 7 days
Oral cefalexin q6h for 7 days and review by GP in 48 hours
IV flucloxacillin q6h monotherapy and ophthalmology review in the morning
MRI orbits in the morning before any antibiotics, to confirm the diagnosis
Urgent contrast CT sinuses/orbits, admit, IV ceftriaxone + flucloxacillin + metronidazole, urgent ophthalmology and ENT
Single dose of oral prednisolone and observation, on the assumption of allergic angioedema
IV cefepime q8h as monotherapy for febrile neutropenia, omitting empirical aminoglycoside
IV ceftriaxone daily plus IV vancomycin six-hourly
IV piperacillin-tazobactam plus IV gentamicin, add vancomycin if line infection suspected or shock persists
IV meropenem eight-hourly as broad-spectrum monotherapy without empirical aminoglycoside
Delay antibiotics until blood cultures return positive to ensure organism-directed therapy today
Intravenous adenosine rapid push at the paediatric SVT dose followed by saline flush
Knee-chest positioning with IV morphine slow push
IV frusemide to offload the right ventricle now
Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion peripherally
Intravenous sodium bicarbonate bolus before any other intervention
Reassure parents and review in 3 months expecting spontaneous closure
Commence oral propranolol three times daily and high-energy formula
Commence oral frusemide BD and an ACE inhibitor with high-energy feeds
Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion to maintain ductal patency
Commence oxygen therapy at 2 L/min via nasal prongs for resting tachypnoea
Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion to maintain ductal patency
Restrict fluids to a conservative ml/kg/day target and continue conservative management
Refer for surgical PDA ligation now as first-line therapy
Course of ibuprofen or indomethacin for pharmacological PDA closure
Commence oral paracetamol six-hourly indefinitely as definitive closure agent
Intravenous frusemide and conservative fluid restriction
Intramuscular vitamin K 1 mg and oral feeds withheld pending surgical review
High-flow nasal cannula oxygen titrated to SpO2 above 95%
Intravenous adenosine rapid push to terminate suspected supraventricular tachycardia
Commence prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) infusion via secure IV access
Crushed ice to the face for 30 seconds
Immediate unsynchronised defibrillation now
Intravenous verapamil by slow IV push
Oral propranolol as a stat dose
Right carotid sinus massage for 10 seconds
Discharge home on paracetamol and ibuprofen for presumed viral illness, review 48 hours
Commence IV ceftriaxone for occult bacterial sepsis and continue cultures
Request transthoracic echocardiogram to look for coronary artery aneurysms before any further treatment decisions
Incomplete Kawasaki disease; treat with IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin and arrange echocardiogram
Commence high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone as monotherapy without IVIG
Discharge home with paracetamol and ibuprofen for simple analgesia and arrange GP review in 48 hours for likely post-viral transient synovitis with planned reassessment
Admit for observation and serial examinations with maintenance IV fluids, withholding empirical antibiotics until a repeat CRP and full blood count at 12 hours have been reviewed
Commence empirical oral flucloxacillin six-hourly and arrange outpatient orthopaedic clinic review the following week with safety-net advice for parents
Arrange urgent ultrasound-guided hip aspiration in theatre, send synovial fluid for cell count and culture, then commence empirical IV flucloxacillin after sampling
Order an urgent contrast MRI of the hip and pelvis and defer joint aspiration and antibiotics until imaging has formally excluded coexisting osteomyelitis or pelvic abscess
Watchful waiting with regular paracetamol for analgesia and structured otoscopic review in 48 hours
Oral amoxicillin three times daily for 7 days
Oral cefuroxime axetil twice daily for a 3-day short course
Topical ciprofloxacin 0.3% ear drops two drops twice daily for 7 days
Intramuscular benzathine benzylpenicillin single weight-based dose at the clinic today
Change current oral amoxicillin to oral amoxicillin/clavulanate twice daily for 10 days and arrange GP review in 48 hours to assess for improvement
Admit, commence intravenous flucloxacillin six-hourly as monotherapy and arrange routine outpatient ENT clinic review within 1 week of discharge home
Admit, commence IV ceftriaxone, request urgent CT temporal bones with contrast and urgent ENT review for consideration of cortical mastoidectomy and myringotomy
Perform urgent lumbar puncture in ED for cell count, protein, glucose and culture and commence empirical IV ceftriaxone for suspected bacterial meningitis complicating mastoiditis
Arrange urgent MRI brain with MR venography to exclude lateral sinus thrombosis and intracranial abscess before commencing any intravenous antibiotic therapy or surgical input
Commence oral amoxicillin three times daily for 10 days as empirical treatment for chronic middle ear effusion and review at the end of the course
Prescribe intranasal fluticasone 50 microgram daily plus oral cetirizine 5 mg daily for 3 months to address presumed allergic contribution to chronic middle-ear effusion
Watchful waiting for a further 6 months without repeat audiology, tympanometry or specialist review given the absence of acute ear pain or discharge
Refer to ENT for consideration of bilateral grommet insertion given persistent bilateral OME >3 months with bilateral conductive hearing loss >25 dB and emerging language and behavioural impact
Refer to a paediatric audiologist for hearing aid fitting as definitive long-term management of the bilateral conductive hearing loss without ENT or grommet consideration
Refer urgently to ENT for examination under general anaesthetic to exclude juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma
Apply topical antiseptic ointment (Naseptin or paraffin) twice daily for 2 weeks plus reinforce first aid technique (pinch the soft part of the nose, lean forward, 10 to 15 minutes)
Perform bedside silver nitrate cautery bilaterally to Little area today with topical antibiotic ointment for 1 week
Insert an anterior Merocel nasal pack into the right nostril and remove at 48 hours in the GP rooms
Commence oral tranexamic acid three times daily for 6 weeks as antifibrinolytic prophylaxis

Oral di/flucloxacillin six-hourly for 7 days now, with incision and drainage if not settling at 48 to 72 hours; refer to ENT for elective complete sinus excision once the acute infection has fully resolved (typically 6 weeks)
Urgent total parotidectomy on suspicion of an infected first branchial cleft anomaly with facial nerve involvement
Reassurance and review only if the lesion enlarges or discharges further
Express the sinus contents by firm squeezing today plus topical mupirocin 2% three times daily for 7 days
Same-day complete excision of the sinus tract under general anaesthesia with antibiotic cover

Send eye swab for Gram stain, bacterial culture and Chlamydia / Neisseria gonorrhoeae PCR, admit, commence IV ceftriaxone or cefotaxime, perform saline eye irrigation and request urgent paediatric ophthalmology review
Prescribe topical chloramphenicol 0.5% eye drops one drop to each eye six-hourly for 7 days and review at the GP clinic the following day to assess improvement
Commence oral azithromycin once daily for 3 days as empirical therapy for likely Chlamydia trachomatis conjunctivitis without admission or specialist input
Reassure the parents that this is most likely chemical (silver nitrate) conjunctivitis from birth, irrigate with normal saline, and review in 48 hours
Apply topical aciclovir 3% eye ointment five times daily for suspected neonatal herpes simplex keratoconjunctivitis and arrange paediatric review the following day
Admit for intravenous ceftriaxone daily, flucloxacillin six-hourly and metronidazole eight-hourly with urgent contrast CT of sinuses and orbits

Commence oral cefalexin or amoxicillin/clavulanate and review at 24 hours with explicit safety-netting for pain on eye movement, restricted movement, proptosis, reduced visual acuity, fever or unwell appearance
Apply warm compresses to the eyelid four-hourly and prescribe topical chloramphenicol 1% eye ointment three-hourly to the affected lid for 7 days without systemic antibiotics
Refer urgently to paediatric ophthalmology for surgical incision and drainage of suspected lid abscess despite the absence of any fluctuant collection on examination today
Prescribe oral prednisolone daily for 5 days alongside oral antibiotics to reduce eyelid swelling more rapidly and accelerate symptom resolution

Activate the paediatric sepsis pathway with a 10 mL/kg crystalloid bolus, draw blood culture and synovial fluid in theatre, then commence empirical IV flucloxacillin plus cefazolin (to cover Kingella kingae) and arrange urgent orthopaedic hip washout under general anaesthetic

Give a 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus over 10 minutes, commence empirical intravenous vancomycin six-hourly and meropenem eight-hourly, and admit to PICU before any orthopaedic intervention or joint aspiration

Transfuse 10 mL/kg of packed red cells immediately for presumed haemodynamic instability and arrange urgent MRI of the left hip and pelvis with contrast before commencing any intravenous antibiotics, fluids or surgical washout for joint source control

Give oral paracetamol for analgesia, observe the child closely in the resuscitation bay for 4 hours and re-examine carefully before deciding on the need for joint aspiration, intravenous antibiotics, fluid resuscitation or surgical washout

Commence intravenous ceftriaxone as empirical monotherapy and defer further fluid resuscitation pending insertion of an arterial line for invasive blood pressure monitoring in PICU before any orthopaedic involvement or aspiration is considered

rewrites._note: mL/kg fluid bolus dosing retained in A and B because the 10 mL/kg vs 20 mL/kg distinction is the discriminating reasoning point (paediatric sepsis pathway change). Drug per-kg doses stripped.

options.text: Cease serotonergics, IV fluids, benzodiazepine sedation, active cooling, admit for monitoring
IV dantrolene with empirical intubation and ICU transfer
IM haloperidol 5 mg for agitation with cardiac monitoring
Withhold sertraline only and continue tramadol with oral paracetamol
Lumbar puncture and empirical IV ceftriaxone for suspected encephalitis
Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.8 g IV every 4 hours until delivery as prophylaxis
Clindamycin 600 mg IV every 8 hours until delivery as monotherapy intrapartum
Vancomycin 1 g IV every 8 hours until delivery as monotherapy intrapartum
Cefazolin 2 g IV loading then 1 g IV every 8 hours until delivery as prophylaxis
Oral amoxicillin 1 g every 8 hours until delivery as intrapartum prophylaxis

Administer oral activated charcoal 50 g now that she is more than 90 minutes from ingestion, place her on continuous cardiac monitoring, and observe with serial ECGs every 30 minutes without any further pharmacological intervention
Intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg
Intravenous flecainide load
Intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 10 minutes
Intravenous sodium bicarbonate bolus with reassessment of QRS, with airway protection and seizure precautions, in consultation with the NSW Poisons Information Centre
Topical mupirocin 2% ointment to lesions three times daily for 5 days, with lesions kept covered or to remain home until lesions are dry; no oral antibiotics
Oral cefalexin up to 500 mg twice daily for 7 days plus daily chlorhexidine washes for the household
Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole twice daily for 5 days as empirical community-acquired MRSA cover
IV flucloxacillin up to 2 g six-hourly with admission for paediatric observation
Reassurance and observation only with return at 7 days if not resolved
Continue hydrocortisone 1% ointment once daily to active eczema and add sedating oral cetirizine 5 mg nightly for pruritus with written instruction to limit moisturiser to dry areas
Step up to methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% ointment daily to active eczema with at least 250 g per week of bland moisturiser and written fingertip-unit instructions
Commence oral prednisolone daily for 7 days to break the flare cycle followed by tapering wean over a further week, continuing hydrocortisone 1% once daily
Start topical tacrolimus 0.03% ointment twice daily to all active areas and stop topical corticosteroids entirely while continuing intermittent emollient as the sole adjunct
Refer urgently to paediatric dermatology this week for systemic immunomodulation with methotrexate or ciclosporin without further trial of stepped-up topical corticosteroid therapy
Intramuscular adrenaline 1:1000 0.3 mg into the mid anterolateral thigh, IV fluid bolus normal saline and admit for cardiorespiratory monitoring overnight
Oral non-sedating antihistamine (e.g. cetirizine), withhold the ibuprofen and amoxicillin, observe for 4 hours and discharge with action plan if stable
Intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg stat plus oral promethazine as a sedating antihistamine, with discharge if asymptomatic at 2 hours after a single dose
Continue both amoxicillin and ibuprofen at current doses, add an oral non-sedating antihistamine for symptom control, and counsel that viral exanthem is the most likely cause
Refer for outpatient intramuscular omalizumab as first-line acute therapy for urticaria with angioedema and discharge after a single dose of oral antihistamine
Oral griseofulvin or terbinafine for at least 4-8 weeks plus a topical antifungal shampoo, with topical/oral antibiotics only if bacterial superinfection is proven
Topical ketoconazole 2% shampoo twice weekly and topical terbinafine 1% cream applied to the scalp lesion daily for 4 weeks with no systemic antifungal therapy
Incision and drainage of the boggy kerion under procedural sedation followed by oral flucloxacillin six-hourly for 7 days with no antifungal cover
Oral prednisolone once daily for 7 days as monotherapy to reduce scalp inflammation, with no antifungal therapy commenced until fungal culture returns
Watchful waiting for 4 weeks pending the fungal culture result with no antifungal therapy in the interim and review only if the lesion enlarges
Isolate the patient immediately, notify the local public health unit on clinical suspicion, and identify and counsel any pregnant contacts about rubella serology
Send rubella IgM and IgG serology and discharge home with no isolation pending the laboratory result before considering notification to the public health unit
Administer MMR vaccination immediately to the patient and to any pregnant contacts in the clinic as urgent post-exposure prophylaxis for rubella
Give intramuscular ceftriaxone empirically pending blood cultures and PCR for meningococcal disease and admit for 24 hours of cardiovascular monitoring
Treat as measles with high-dose oral vitamin A and MMR post-exposure prophylaxis for unvaccinated household contacts within 72 hours of exposure
Discharge home now with oral cetirizine 10 mg daily, a written ASCIA Action Plan for Allergic Reactions, a single in-date EpiPen and follow-up review in primary care within 48 hours of discharge

Observe in the ED short-stay unit for exactly 4 hours from the last adrenaline dose and discharge if asymptomatic with the standard discharge bundle and no extended observation despite severity features

Admit for at least 6-12 hours of monitored observation given severe features (two adrenaline doses, asthma, transient collapse) and discharge with updated Anaphylaxis Action Plan, two in-date adrenaline autoinjectors, ICS optimisation and immunology referral

Admit to PICU for 24 hours of intubated airway monitoring with a continuous intravenous adrenaline infusion titrated to MAP and prophylactic salbutamol nebuliser regardless of current clinical stability
Discharge after 1 hour of observation with a 3-day course of oral prednisolone as routine pharmacological prevention of a biphasic reaction without extended ED observation
Send serum tryptase during the current attack and commence intravenous adrenaline infusion with hydrocortisone as for refractory anaphylaxis unresponsive to intramuscular adrenaline

Send C1 inhibitor antigenic level and function (and consider SERPING1 testing); treat acute attack with C1 inhibitor concentrate (or icatibant) and arrange immunology follow-up for long-term prophylaxis planning
Send total IgE and a paediatric food-specific IgE panel and commence daily non-sedating antihistamine prophylaxis with up-titration as needed for symptom control
Refer for contrast-enhanced abdominal CT and outpatient gastroenterology workup for the recurrent colicky abdominal pain in isolation without further immunology investigation
Commence daily oral tranexamic acid as immediate definitive long-term prophylaxis without C1 inhibitor testing or specialist immunology input at this presentation
Continue carbamazepine at the current dose, treat as a self-limiting viral exanthem with supportive paracetamol and oral cetirizine, and review in clinic in 7 days

Stop carbamazepine immediately, admit for organ-function monitoring, commence systemic corticosteroid (e.g. prednisolone) and consult dermatology and immunology; document the reaction and avoid aromatic anticonvulsants
Diagnose Stevens-Johnson syndrome based on fever and rash and arrange transfer to a tertiary burns unit for crystalloid fluid resuscitation and burns-style dressing care
Diagnose incomplete Kawasaki disease, commence IVIG 2 g/kg and high-dose aspirin and obtain urgent echocardiography to exclude coronary artery aneurysms
Switch carbamazepine to phenytoin at an equivalent maintenance dose and monitor liver function tests weekly without specific dermatology or immunology referral
Continue cetirizine 10 mg daily at the standard dose and add a 6-week course of oral prednisolone daily with no taper, then reassess for biologic therapy
Up-titrate the non-sedating H1 antihistamine to up to fourfold the standard dose (e.g. cetirizine 40 mg/day or equivalent) and review at 4 weeks before considering omalizumab
Add a sedating antihistamine such as promethazine at bedtime as the primary add-on therapy for chronic urticaria refractory to standard-dose cetirizine
Start intramuscular adrenaline 0.3 mg twice weekly as suppressive therapy until the wheals subside, with a written ASCIA Anaphylaxis Action Plan for school staff
Commence ciclosporin in divided doses as the preferred next-line agent before any consideration of antihistamine up-titration or biologic omalizumab therapy
Continue oral aciclovir six-hourly, observe in the short-stay unit for 4 hours and discharge home if the pain settles with simple analgesia and reassurance

Resuscitate with crystalloid, administer empirical broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering Group A streptococcus and Staphylococcus aureus (including clindamycin for toxin suppression) and arrange urgent surgical assessment for source control
Apply topical mupirocin 2% ointment three times daily to the dusky area and commence oral cefalexin six-hourly for presumed impetiginised varicella lesions
Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg as definitive monotherapy for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome without empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics or surgical input
Obtain urgent MRI of the left thigh with contrast to characterise fascial involvement before commencing intravenous antibiotics, fluid resuscitation or surgical consultation

Cease trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole immediately, transfer to a centre with paediatric burns/intensive-care capability with ophthalmology and dermatology input, commence supportive care (fluid resuscitation, temperature control, analgesia, sterile non-adherent dressings, mucosal care), and document drug allergy and consider specialist-directed immunomodulation

Continue trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole at half dose, commence oral prednisolone daily with topical chlorhexidine 0.05% to mucosal surfaces twice daily, and admit for 24 hours of fluid balance and pain monitoring before reassessment

Treat as staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome with intravenous flucloxacillin six-hourly plus encourage oral hydration with maintenance fluids, paracetamol for fever and observation in the ED short-stay unit for 24 hours before discharge

Diagnose incomplete Kawasaki disease, commence intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 12 hours and high-dose aspirin in four divided doses with echocardiography on day 3 of admission to exclude coronary aneurysms

Perform a 4 mm punch biopsy of involved skin in ED, send for routine histology and direct immunofluorescence, and defer all therapy including culprit drug withdrawal and transfer until the histology result is available
Transfer to theatre for Ramstedt pyloromyotomy after a single saline bolus without first correcting the alkalosis or chloride

Resuscitate with 0.9% saline, then maintenance and replacement with 0.45% saline plus 5% dextrose plus potassium until Cl over 100, HCO3 under 30, K over 3.5 mmol/L; confirmatory ultrasound; then pyloromyotomy
Commence omeprazole orally and review in clinic in one week as outpatient reflux management
Request urgent upper GI contrast study to exclude malrotation before any fluid resuscitation or surgical referral
Trial extensively hydrolysed formula for 2 weeks as cow's milk protein allergy is the most likely diagnosis
Commence oral omeprazole daily for a 4-week trial as the most likely diagnosis is GORD-related vomiting
Switch to extensively hydrolysed formula for 2-4 weeks, monitor symptoms/growth, re-challenge to confirm; allergy referral if severe
Switch directly to soy-based formula as the first-line alternative formula without considering hydrolysate today
Order skin-prick testing and specific IgE to cow's milk to confirm the diagnosis before any dietary change
Advise the mother to dilute the formula with extra water and add infant gripe water at each feed
Continue lifestyle measures alone and recheck urine ACR in six months' time
Commence high-dose IV cyclophosphamide induction immediately without further investigation
Commence high-dose oral prednisolone daily as first-line agent today
Refer for elective tonsillectomy as the patient's definitive disease-modifying therapy
Maximise ACE inhibitor or ARB, targeting BP < 120/70 mmHg and proteinuria < 1 g/day
Oral prednisolone alone titrated to symptom control over weeks
IV methylprednisolone then oral prednisolone, plus rituximab (or cyclophosphamide), with PJP prophylaxis
Therapeutic plasma exchange alone as the sole intervention for induction
Oral mycophenolate mofetil 1 g twice daily as monotherapy for induction
Await renal biopsy result before starting any disease-modifying immunosuppression
Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily orally for 7 days post-discharge
Mechanical thromboprophylaxis with TED stockings alone for duration of admission
Therapeutic-dose enoxaparin 90 mg twice daily for 6 weeks postpartum
Prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily for at least 10 days postoperatively
Aspirin 100 mg orally daily for 6 weeks postpartum as VTE prophylaxis
